sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 29, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE didn't collect the target - it just created a G frame at rows 24-27, cols 36-39 and shrank the white block to col 40. The target at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 is still uncollected. White block is to the LEFT of green, target is to the RIGHT. Need white block adjacent to target.
- Goal model: Collect all three blue-square targets by moving adjacent and pressing SPACE.
- Action model: Movement moves player 4 cells. SPACE collects adjacent targets.
- Plan: Try moving LEFT to swap white block to the RIGHT side of green, making it adjacent to the target.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 28 of about 71 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
LEFT was blocked (0 cells changed). The G frame at rows 24-27, cols 36-39 is blocking LEFT movement. So I can't move LEFT.
Let me try moving DOWN to get below the target, then approach from below.
Current: green rows 24-27, cols 41-43
Target: rows 24-27, cols 44-47
If I move DOWN: green rows 28-31, cols 41-43
Then move RIGHT: green rows 28-31, cols 45-47 (but this overlaps with target at rows 24-27, cols 44-47... no, different rows)
Then move UP: green rows 24-27, cols 45-47 (overlaps with target!)
Wait, but the target is at cols 44-47. If green is at cols 45-47, they overlap. But the question is whether the white block would be adjacent.
If I move UP, the white block would be above the green block. So white would be at rows 20-23, cols 45-47. The target is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47. The white block would be above the target, not overlapping or adjacent.
Hmm, that doesn't work either.
Let me think about this differently. The white block needs to be adjacent to the target. The target is at rows 24-27, cols 44-47.
- If white is above target: rows 20-23, cols 44-47 (adjacent)
- If white is below target: rows 28-31, cols 44-47 (adjacent)
- If white is left of target: rows 24-27, cols 40-43 (adjacent)
- If white is right of target: rows 24-27, cols 48-51 (adjacent)
Currently white is at rows 24-27, col 40. This is to the left of the target but with a gap (cols 41-43 is green).
To get white above the target, I need to move UP. But the player is at rows 24-27, and moving UP would put the player at rows 20-23. The white block would be above that, at rows 16-19. That's too far above the target.
Wait, I think I'm confusing the white block's behavior. Let me reconsider.
When the player moves, the white block moves WITH the player. The white block is always adjacent to the green block on one side. The side depends on the last movement direction.
Looking at the current state:
- Green: rows 24-27, cols 41-43
- White: rows 24-27, col 40
The white is a 4-cell strip at col 40, rows 24-27. The green is a 12-cell block at rows 24-27, cols 41-43.
So the white block is a 4x1 strip to the LEFT of the green block.
If I move DOWN, the green block goes to rows 28-31, cols 41-43. The white block would be... where? If the last movement was RIGHT (which put white on the LEFT), then moving DOWN might keep white on the LEFT, or move it to the TOP.
Actually, I think the white block's position is determined by the direction of the LAST movement. When moving RIGHT, white is on the LEFT. When moving UP, white is on the TOP (above). When moving DOWN, white is on the BOTTOM (below). When moving LEFT, white is on the RIGHT.
So if I move DOWN, the white block should be BELOW the green block. Then:
- Green: rows 28-31, cols 41-43
- White: rows 32-35, cols 41-43 (below green)
But wait, there's a 'c' frame at rows 32-35, cols 16-19. That's far to the left, so it shouldn't interfere.
Then if I move RIGHT:
- Green: rows 28-31, cols 45-47
- White: rows 32-35, cols 45-47 (below green, since last movement was DOWN... or does RIGHT change it to LEFT?)
Hmm, I'm not sure about the white block's behavior. Let me just try moving DOWN and see what happens.
Actually, let me try a different approach. What if I move DOWN first, then RIGHT, then UP? This would position the player to the right of the target, with the white block potentially adjacent.
Let me try: DOWN, RIGHT, UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: LEFT is blocked by G frame at rows 24-27, cols 36-39. Player stuck at rows 24-27, cols 41-43 with white at col 40. Target at rows 24-27, cols 44-47 still uncollected. Need to reposition so white block is adjacent to target.
Plan: Try DOWN to move below the target row, then RIGHT and UP to approach from a different angle.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try DOWN, RIGHT, UP to approach target from different angle
action(['DOWN', 'RIGHT', 'UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
